story:
Jen lives by the unwritten rules for the single woman who wants a child: don’t look at children, don’t engage, don’t talk about them, don’t let others talk about them, and absolutely don’t confess that you want one. Any glance, any story, any softness around babies broadcasts your yearning like a tinny loudspeaker and turns you, in the mind of any would‑be suitor, from possibility to problem.

So Fridays become her private rebellion. On her half day, she slips into a museum housed in a former church and sits before a marble mother and child—the Madonna, serene, nursing. Stone that feels like breath. A virgin, a mother, perfect and untouchable. From her first visit in early spring she loves Her, though at first she hurries past, afraid passersby will relish the pathos: a childless woman gazing at the mother of mothers. Still, she returns almost weekly, allowing herself to look openly, to feel the awe and ache she hides everywhere else.

Jen’s childlessness feels to her like something visible and damp, soaking everything; she believes it’s the first thing people see, the thing they instantly forgive with modern politeness, that flashing pity more humiliating than scorn. She tries to mask it and fails. Everyone forgives her. Everyone sees. Everyone but him.

He’s wiry, a little ugly, smelling like too much sports deodorant. He looks at the statue, at Jen, back at the statue, and squints. “Is she bumming you out, Jen?” he asks in a raspy voice. She stiffens. “Excuse me?” He keeps going, casual, untrained in the rituals of polite distance. “Why are you looking at her if she’s making you sad?” She reaches for hauteur: it’s art, she says; art is supposed to make you sad. He doesn’t play along. He muses about stone faces and missing eyelashes, suggests fake ones, jokes that his own are fake. She, mortified by her own mouth, compliments his eyelashes, then backpedals. He smiles, and she feels that brief, ridiculous flare of attraction she now feels toward anyone with the potential to end her longing.

He asks again, simply: why does this make you sad?

She wants to disappear into marble herself—frozen, voiceless, unhurtable. She prepares to drive him off with the blunt force of her truth. But when it comes out it’s softer than she intended: “Because I want a baby, and she has one.”

She waits for the choreography she knows by heart: the shuffle, the watch check, the urgent call, the apology formed from manners and exit strategies. She can already feel him scolding himself for intruding, for missing the obvious, for failing to pre‑forgive her.

Instead he gathers nothing but warmth. “Well,” he says, almost cheerful, “if you think that baby’s great, wait till you see the size of the horse some soldier has in the next room. Come on, I’ll show you.” His smile is less bright now, but steadier. He doesn’t fix or pity or flee. He redirects.

Jen stands. They walk together toward the marble horse.
